ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists for dry-tossed noodle with fried pork chop specifically; mapped to broader 'noodle dish' class (FOODON_00002473).
ontology.foodex2_description: Noodles, cooked — used as the closest FoodEx2 anchor for this wheat-noodle-based dish
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for dry-tossed noodle with fried pork chop; A0EJW (cooked noodles) is the closest defensible broader anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for this specific dish; not assigned to avoid fabrication.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 350 g, representing a single plate of dry noodles with one fried pork chop as typically served at a Singapore hawker stall.

nutrient_source.title: 干拌排骨面 Dry Noodle Tossed In Special Sauce With Fried Pork Chop — HPB Singapore Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for this dish (cr_id G00010062). All per-100g values are taken verbatim from the HPB record. Saturated fat is not reported by HPB for this entry and is estimated from USDA FoodData Central data for deep-fried pork chop (USDA SR Legacy #10062) at approximately 3.0 g/100g for the composite dish.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: The HPB database reports carbohydrate_g as 0.0 g/100g for this dish entry, which is anomalous for a noodle dish and likely reflects a data limitation in the HPB record (possibly reflecting only the pork chop component). Because the HPB-reported carbohydrate is 0.0 g/100g, the glycemic load per serving calculates to 0.0. The GI of 52 is assigned as an analogous estimate based on the University of Sydney GI Database entry for wheat noodles (boiled, GI ~52, medium-low), reflecting the noodle component of the dish. GL per serving = (52 × 0.0 × 350) / (100 × 100) = 0.0 based on the HPB-reported carbohydrate figure. This anomaly is documented in notes.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 1050 mg/100g — far exceeds the 600 mg/100g upper threshold
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit this dish; sodium at 1050 mg/100g is 75% of the 1400 mg daily target in a single 350 g serving, and patients should request reduced sauce and avoid adding condiments.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Refined wheat noodles contribute high glycaemic load; deep-fried pork chop adds saturated fat worsening insulin resistance
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit this dish due to the refined wheat noodle base (typically GI ~52, high carbohydrate load per serving) and the deep-fried pork chop which contributes saturated fat that impairs insulin sensitivity; request wholemeal or lower-GI noodle alternatives and grilled rather than fried pork.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High sodium (1050 mg/100g), saturated fat from deep-fried pork chop, and cholesterol (89 mg/100g) collectively elevate cardiovascular risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid frequent consumption of this dish; the combination of very high sodium, saturated fat from deep-frying, and dietary cholesterol at 89 mg/100g raises blood pressure and LDL cholesterol, and patients should choose steamed or grilled lean protein alternatives with reduced-sauce noodles.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying generates pro-inflammatory AGEs; pork arachidonic acid amplifies inflammatory cytokines; sodium exceeds RA threshold
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this dish due to deep-fried pork generating advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) that worsen joint inflammation, pork-derived arachidonic acid amplifying inflammatory cytokines, and sodium at 1050 mg/100g exceeding the RA threshold; grilled or steamed chicken or tofu substitutions are recommended.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Dry Noodle Tossed in Special Sauce with Fried Pork Chop (干拌排骨面, Gān Bàn Páigǔ Miàn) is a popular Chinese-Singaporean hawker dish consisting of wheat egg noodles that are boiled, drained, and tossed in a fragrant special sauce typically composed of soy sauce, oyster sauce, sesame oil, and seasoning sauce, then topped with spring onions and served alongside a deep-fried pork chop. Unlike soup noodle dishes, this is a dry preparation — the noodles are coated in sauce rather than submerged in broth. The dish is served without accompanying soup. The texture is springy and chewy from the wheat noodles, with a savoury, umami-rich coating and a crispy, juicy fried pork chop. Clinically, this dish is high concern for hypertension (sodium 1050 mg/100g), Type 2 Diabetes (refined carbohydrate noodle base, deep-fried protein), and cardiovascular disease (high sodium, saturated fat, cholesterol 89 mg/100g). The HPB-reported carbohydrate of 0.0 g/100g is anomalous and likely reflects only the pork chop component; the full dish as consumed contains significant carbohydrate from the noodles. Protein is 19.1 g/100g and fat is 10.5 g/100g per HPB data. Commonly found at Singapore hawker centres and coffee shops.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id G00010062, confidence 0.95), which is the primary approved source for Singapore dishes. The carbohydrate anomaly (0.0 g/100g) is documented and does not reduce confidence in the HPB provenance; it reflects the HPB record as published.
notes: CARBOHYDRATE ZERO VALUE: carbohydrate_g is reported as 0.0 g/100g by the HPB Singapore Foodo database for this entry. This is anomalous for a noodle dish and likely reflects that the HPB nutrient analysis was conducted on the pork chop component only, or that the carbohydrate contribution of the noodles was not captured in this specific HPB record. The value is preserved as reported by HPB (confirmed zero per HPB source) but users and clinicians should be aware that the full dish as consumed contains significant carbohydrate from wheat noodles (typically 25–40 g per 100g of cooked noodles). sugar_g is 0.0 and dietary_fibre_g is 0.0 — both confirmed zero per HPB source for this entry, consistent with the anomalous carbohydrate figure. NEGATIVE SENTINEL VALUES: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, vitamin profile, phosphorus, zinc, selenium, and moisture data for this composite hawker dish are not available in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central for this specific preparation as of 2026. SATURATED FAT: saturated_fat_g estimated at 3.0 g/100g based on USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for deep-fried pork chop (closest approved-source analogue); HPB does not report saturated fat for this entry. GI NOTE: GI of 52 is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database data for boiled wheat noodles; glycemic load per serving calculates to 0.0 based on the HPB-reported carbohydrate of 0.0 g/100g, which is anomalous as noted above.
